3A_Bibliographic.citation: Alexander, J. D., Linkersdorfer, J., Toda-Thorne, K., Sullivan, R. M., Cummins, K. M., Tomko, R. L., Allen, N. B., Bagot, K. S., Fuemmeler, B. F., Baker, F. C., Kiss, O., Mason, M. J., Nguyen-Louie, T. T., Tapert, S. F., Smith, C. J., Squeglia, L. M., & Wade, N. E. (2024). Passively sensing smartphone use in teens with rates of use by sex and across operating systems. Scientific Reports, 14, 17982.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Here we examine cross-sectional data from ABCD Data Release 5.0, collected between September 2019 through January 2021, containing the first half of Year 4 follow-up data (total n=4,754; a subset, n=1,463, had usable ABCD-EARS data).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Passively sensed smartphone use (ABCD-EARS): average daily keystrokes and average daily app use (minutes), plus self-reported screen time
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD-EARS (Effortless Assessment of Risk States) app for passive sensing; ABCD Youth Screen Time Questionnaire for self-report
3C_Exposure.modalities: smartphone use overall, social media apps, gaming apps, entertainment apps, photography apps, and other app categories
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (average daily minutes of app use, average daily keystrokes; self-reported hours/day)
3C_Exposure.time_frame: 3 weeks of passive sensing; typical weekday/weekend (self-report)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The ABCD-EARS application recorded the time of each keystroke and the application in the foreground. Average number of keystrokes recorded and minutes of app use by a participant per day by app category and overall are reported. All participants in the ABCD Study completed annual surveys on their SMA, including screen-based activities across all devices, excluding time spent on schoolwork.
3C_Exposure.notes: This is primarily a measurement/validation paper, not an exposure-outcome study.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Measurement properties of passive smartphone sensing (convergent validity, reliability); descriptive patterns of smartphone use
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: ABCD-EARS passive sensing measures (app use, keystrokes); self-reported screen time for validation
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: This is a measurement validation study. Primary outcomes include ICCs for reliability, correlations between passive and self-report measures, and descriptive statistics of smartphone use patterns by time of day, app category, sex, and operating system.
3D_Outcome.notes: No traditional health outcome. This is a methods/measurement paper.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Descriptive statistics, Pearson correlations, ICCs, Cronbach's alpha, beta regressions, two-sample t-tests with bootstrapped CIs, Bland-Altman plots
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: None in primary analyses (descriptive/validation); sex and operating system as grouping variables
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: All analyses were run in RStudio using R version 4.2.3. We computed both intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs; single random raters) and Cronbach's alphas for daily app use and daily keyboard use. We computed Pearson correlation coefficients between average daily keystrokes, average daily app use, and self-reported smartphone use. Two-sample t-tests were used to test whether summary measures of screen time differed as a function of participant sex or operating system.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Pearson correlation between passive sensing and self-report (Android users)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: self-reported smartphone use modestly correlated with average daily app use (r_473 = 0.35, p < 0.001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: EARS average daily app use (correlation, Android users)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Pearson correlation between two passive measures
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: average daily keystrokes and average daily app usage were modestly correlated (r_475 = 0.33, p < 0.001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Self-reported smartphone use (correlation, iOS and Android)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Range of Pearson correlations across iOS and Android
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: self-reported smartphone use was modestly correlated with average daily keystrokes (r_s_461-1395 = 0.13-0.21, ps < 0.001)

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: This is a measurement paper focused on describing passive sensing data; effect size discussions are not applicable in the same way as exposure-outcome studies.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Parents may wish to limit their teen's phone access particularly overnight, given the prevalence of smartphone use at that time. Teens themselves may benefit from feedback on how much they are using their devices, given underreporting which may indicate a lack of self-awareness of their use.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion is methodologically focused, describing the validity and reliability of passive smartphone sensing measures. Key findings include that teens use phones about 2 hours more per day than they self-report, social media and texting dominate use, and females log more smartphone use than males. The paper recommends parents limit overnight phone access and suggests teens may benefit from usage feedback. The tone is descriptive rather than alarmist, though it does note that adolescent girls may be especially at risk given higher social media use.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: This is a measurement/methods paper rather than a traditional exposure-outcome study. Coding health outcome fields is somewhat forced since the primary aim is describing and validating passive sensing measures.
